  Autonomy, task significance, complexity, feedback, and social support are all strongly related to satisfaction and performance. This weakens an “interestingness almost exclusively” rule because social context and autonomy are also major drivers.

- **“The Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan et al.**  
  Meaningful work is robustly associated with engagement, job satisfaction, life satisfaction, performance, and lower withdrawal intentions. This supports prioritizing mission and impact, though meaningful work is not identical to technical novelty.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge et al.**

- **“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton**  
  Higher income predicts better life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed little improvement beyond a comfortable threshold in this study. This is evidence against heavily overweighting salary once needs, security, and options are covered.

- **“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth**  
  Experience-sampling data showed experienced well-being continuing to rise with income above earlier proposed thresholds. This suggests money still matters, but the returns are gradual compared with large differences in daily work.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers**  
  This reconciliation finds income generally predicts higher well-being, but the relationship varies across the happiness distribution. It argues against both extremes: salary is not irrelevant, and salary is not usually the main determinant of happiness.

- **“Managerial Work: Analysis from Observation” — Mintzberg**  
  Managerial work is often fragmented, interrupt-driven, verbal, and heavily interpersonal. This is relevant because leading a team may satisfy a leadership goal while reducing deep technical research time.

**“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath (1999)**  
People tend to overestimate how much others are motivated by pay and underestimate intrinsic motivation. This supports the idea that interesting work can be socially underrated, though not that it should dominate every other factor.

**“The ‘What’ and ‘Why’ of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior” — Deci & Ryan (2000)**  
Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central drivers of motivation and well-being. Interesting work matters most when it also supports these needs.

**“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interestingness” is important, but leadership scope could matter if it increases autonomy, significance, and feedback.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover intentions. This argues for comparing whole-role fit rather than optimizing only salary or only intellectual interest.

**“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis” — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds (2020)**  
People are generally more satisfied when their work matches their vocational interests. The relationship is meaningful but not all-explaining, so interest should be heavily weighted but not treated as the only variable.

**“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover” — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka & Lanivich (2011)**  
Vocational interests predict job performance and turnover beyond some standard selection variables. This supports taking “will I find the work interesting?” seriously as a performance and retention question.

**“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**  
Intrinsic motivation predicts performance strongly, especially quality of performance. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence favors balance rather than an exclusive focus on interesting work.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
Pay level has only a modest association with job satisfaction. This suggests a higher salary is relevant but often weaker than day-to-day work quality and fit.

**“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006)**  
People often overestimate how much income affects daily happiness because money becomes salient during comparison. This supports the idea that salary can be overweighted relative to everyday work experience.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)**  
Experienced well-being generally rises with income, mostly in a roughly logarithmic way, with plateauing mainly among the least happy subgroup. This tempers the claim that money barely matters, while still implying diminishing marginal returns.

**“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay (2019)**  
Meaningful work is associated with greater job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, life meaning, and lower withdrawal intentions. Most evidence is correlational, so it supports meaningful work as important rather than proving it should override everything else.

**“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson (2009)**  
People with a strong calling experience unusually high meaning and identity from work. They may also accept sacrifice, poor conditions, or lower rewards, showing that passion can have costs.

**“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018)**  
Believing interests are fixed can reduce openness and persistence when work becomes difficult. This challenges a simplistic “only follow current interest” view and suggests interests can develop in new roles.

**“The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout” — Demerouti, Bakker, Nachreiner & Schaufeli (2001)**  
Burnout depends on the balance between job demands and resources such as autonomy, support, and feedback. Interesting work can still burn you out, while less inherently fascinating work can be sustainable if resources are high.

**“Predictors of Objective and Subjective Career Success: A Meta-Analysis” — Ng, Eby, Sorensen & Feldman (2005)**  
Objective career success and subjective career success have overlapping but different predictors. Pay and leadership can build objective career capital, while subjective success depends more on fit, values, and satisfaction.

**On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives**  
People tend to overestimate how much others are motivated by extrinsic incentives and underestimate intrinsic and social motives. This supports the claim that interesting or meaningful work is often underrated, but not that it should be the only factor.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
Autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity strongly predict job satisfaction, motivation, performance, and lower strain. This supports heavily weighting interesting and autonomous work, while also showing that social context and work conditions matter.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
Person–job and person–organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. The evidence favors choosing the role that fits your interests, values, skills, and desired identity rather than following a universal “maximize interestingness” rule.

**Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model**  
People make more progress and gain more well-being from goals that are personally endorsed rather than externally pressured. This supports prioritizing work you genuinely find meaningful or interesting over choices driven mainly by salary, prestige, or others’ expectations.

**The “What” and “Why” of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior**  
Activities that satisfy autonomy, competence, and relatedness are linked to stronger motivation and well-being than controlled or purely extrinsic pursuits. Interesting research may satisfy autonomy and competence, while team leadership may satisfy competence and relatedness if it is genuinely self-endorsed.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
Tangible rewards, especially when experienced as controlling, can undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks people already find interesting. This does not mean higher salary is bad, but it cautions against letting marginal compensation dominate intrinsically motivating work.

**Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do**  
Creative performance is strongly supported by intrinsic motivation, challenge, autonomy, resources, and supportive management. For research-heavy careers, interesting work is not just a perk; it can be an input to creativity, learning, and long-run output.

**Interest—The Curious Emotion**  
Interest motivates exploration, learning, attention, and engagement with novel or complex material. This makes interestingness especially relevant for research roles, though the paper does not imply it should override every other career attribute.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
Pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This supports not overweighting a somewhat higher salary once compensation is acceptable and fair.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion**  
People often overestimate the effect of income on happiness because income is salient when evaluating life choices. This supports the idea that day-to-day work experience, including whether the work is engaging, can be underrated relative to salary.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved**  
Emotional well-being generally continues rising with income for many people, though the relationship varies across individuals. This is evidence against ignoring salary entirely, even if it does not make salary the dominant factor.

**The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages**  
Workers are willing to trade meaningful amounts of pay for nonpay job attributes such as flexibility, autonomy, pace, and other working conditions. This shows nonpecuniary job features have large economic value, but the right tradeoff differs across individuals.

**Job Satisfaction and Promotions**  
Promotions and expected promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction, partly through status, growth, and future opportunity. This suggests that leading a small team could be a real benefit if it matches your long-term aspirations, even if the work is less intrinsically interesting.
